does: This paper develops a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing how well large language models communicate about climate change. The framework, grounded in science communication research, evaluates LLM responses across eight dimensions and thirty specific issues, examining both presentational quality (clarity, tone, structure) and epistemological adequacy (scientific accuracy, appropriate uncertainty, proper reasoning). The authors evaluate multiple recent LLMs on diverse climate questions and introduce a novel scalable oversight protocol combining AI assistance with human raters possessing relevant scientific education. Their results reveal a critical gap between surface-level presentation quality, where LLMs often perform well, and deeper epistemological soundness, where significant deficiencies emerge in how models handle scientific uncertainty, justify claims, and communicate domain-specific concepts accurately.
connects: This work builds on established science communication research that emphasizes the importance of both clarity and scientific fidelity in public-facing explanations. It extends recent efforts to systematically evaluate LLM capabilities in specialized domains beyond standard benchmarks, responding to growing concerns about model reliability in high-stakes applications. The paper combines insights from literature on human-AI collaboration and scalable oversight—areas that have explored how AI assistance and expert human judgment can be effectively integrated. It also connects to broader research on LLM evaluation methodologies, which have increasingly moved beyond simple accuracy metrics toward more nuanced frameworks capturing multiple dimensions of quality. The climate domain selection reflects recognition that critical real-world problems demand rigorous assessment of whether LLMs can reliably augment human expertise.
enables: This framework and evaluation protocol establish foundations for assessing AI communication quality in other scientifically critical domains, from health information to environmental science to public health. The scalable oversight approach combining AI assistance with educated raters offers a template for evaluating LLMs where expert human judgment is essential but expensive. Results highlighting the gap between surface and epistemological qualities point toward needed improvements in LLM training and prompting strategies that could emphasize scientific reasoning and uncertainty quantification. The work enables future research into whether targeted interventions—such as retrieval-augmented generation, specialized fine-tuning on climate data, or improved reasoning mechanisms—can close identified epistemic gaps. It also supports policy discussions about when and how LLMs can responsibly serve as information sources or decision-support tools in climate-related contexts.
